content:
Are there any ideas about how string manipulation will be done with kernel Rust? I suppose std::string::String wasn't pulled in - I'd guess it contains some panic!(), e.g. when failing to allocate memory.

To throw in one specific example, I was trying to generate firmware paths depending on the chipset, where the chipset is represented by an enum. Since strum_macros isn't available, I thought I just go with derive(Debug) and format!(). But we don't have format!() (cause we don't have std::string::String I guess). I can use fmt!() instead, but this does give me a CString, which doesn't seem to support any string manipulations like to_lowercase(), etc.
String is not supported because it doesn't allow for fallible allocations, but I don't see why we can't add a to_lowercase for CString.

Ariel Miculas said:

Have you tried format_args?

IIUC, this doesn't provide anything additional to me than using fmt!() directly, does it?

Ariel Miculas said:

String is not supported because it doesn't allow for fallible allocations, but I don't see why we can't add a to_lowercase for CString.

Sure, I'm happy to do that. I just want to see if there are any plans already.
I remember having some issues withfmt!, but it seems to do the same thing as format_args.
I wonder if we care about the full UTF-8 conversion for upper- / lowercase? ASCII should be enough I guess?
CString is ASCII, though, so I'm not sure where UTF-8 comes into discussion

I suppose std::string::String wasn't pulled in

We had String in the rust branch, but unless/until someone needs it, we don't need to add it upstream.

I wonder if we care about the full UTF-8 conversion for upper- / lowercase? ASCII should be enough I guess?

CString may not be UTF-8. In any case, it perhaps could be called to_ascii_lowercase/ make_ascii_lowercase like in str if needed.

However, what would you think the use case would be? i.e. we typically need a use case (or ideally a user) to upstream code.

We had String in the rust branch, but unless/until someone needs it, we don't need to add it upstream.

Dealing with strings and doing some kind of String manipulation is probably a common enough use case.

Plus there may be use cases like [1] and [2].

CString may not be UTF-8. In any case, it perhaps could be called to_ascii_lowercase/ make_ascii_lowercase like in str if needed.

Yeah, that's what I thought of as well.

However, what would you think the use case would be? i.e. we typically need a use case (or ideally a user) to upstream code.

As mentioned above I want to generate firmware paths from enum values representing the chipset. It's for the Nova driver project (Nouveau successor for GSP based GPUs) which we're ramping up currently. E.g.

#[derive(Debug)] // TODO use strum_macros instead?
pub(crate) enum Chipset {
    TU102 = 0x162,
    [...]
}

let chip_name = CString::try_from_fmt(fmt!("{:?}", self.spec.chipset))?.to_lowercase();
let fw_gsp_path = CString::try_from_fmt(fmt!("nvidia/{}/gsp/gsp-535.113.01.bin", &*chip_name))?;

Personally, I'm fine with just expanding CString. Though, maybe we want to define whether we see it as the way to deal with strings in kernel Rust in general, or just a (lightweight) wrapper for c strings to avoid duplicate infrastructure.

[1] https://godbolt.org/z/EddYPEfc1
[2] https://godbolt.org/z/b3cTvMbqj

Disclaimer: Never wrote Rust code before, hence I might just not know what I'm doing.

Dealing with strings and doing some kind of String manipulation is probably a common enough use case.

Plus there may be use cases like [1] and [2].

Yeah, I was talking about String specifically, not general/basic string manipulation.

As mentioned above I want to generate firmware paths from enum values representing the chipset. It's for the Nova driver project (Nouveau successor for GSP based GPUs) which we're ramping up currently.

Nice!

Personally, I'm fine with just expanding CString. Though, maybe we want to define whether we see it as the way to deal with strings in kernel Rust in general, or just a (lightweight) wrapper for c strings to avoid duplicate infrastructure.

I guess we will probably use/need both.

Have you seen bstr crate from Andrew Gallant ? It's just a wrapper over [u8] (or Vec<u8>) which expects utf-8 but doesn't assume it's valid, I think something like that may be useful in the kernel rather than a full utf8 String.
